"Sayyidun Abdullh ibn Umar    was among the most devoted, scrupulous, and steadfast Companions of the Messenger of Allh . He was a man whose life became a living embodiment of the Sunnah, so precise in his adherence that he would follow even the smallest actions of the Prophet  out of love and reverence.

He was Abdullh ibn Umar ibn al-Khab al-Adaw al-Qurash, the son of the great Caliph Sayyidun Umar ibn al-Khab   . He was born in Makkah before the Hijrah and embraced Islm at a young age alongside his noble father. He emigrated to al-Madnah al-Munawwarah at the age of ten, and thus was counted among the early Muhjirn, those whom Allh, Most High, praised in His Book:

And the foremost, the first of the Muhjirn and the Anr and those who followed them with excellence, Allh is pleased with them, and they are pleased with Him, and He has prepared for them gardens beneath which rivers flow, wherein they will abide forever. That is the great success.

[Qurn 9:100]

In his youth, he longed to participate in battle alongside the Messenger of Allh . He was presented at Badr but was turned back due to his young age. He was again presented at Uud, when he was only thirteen years old, hoping to join the ranks, but he was sent back by the Prophet  due to his youth, alongside Sayyidun Usmah ibn Zayd   . That moment weighed heavily upon him, and he later said: 

A night like that never came my way, filled with sleeplessness, sadness, and weeping, because the Messenger of Allh  did not accept me.

After returning him from Uud, the Messenger of Allh  appointed him among those tasked with guarding the children in Madnah. 

Yet his heart remained attached to striving in the path of Allh, and his sincere supplication to be permitted to fight was soon answered. In the fifth year after Hijrah, when he reached the age of fifteen, he was allowed to participate in the Battle of al-Khandaq. 

From that point forward, he took part in the major campaigns of Islm. He participated in the campaign against Ban Qurayah, the journey to al-udaybiyyah, and was among those who pledged beneath the tree in the Pledge of al-Riwn, a moment honored by Allh, Most High:

Indeed, Allh was pleased with the believers when they pledged allegiance to you under the tree. He knew what was in their hearts, so He sent down tranquility upon them and rewarded them with a near victory.

[Qurn 48:18]

He later joined the campaigns of Khaybar, Mutah, the Conquest of Makkah, the expedition against Ban Judhaymah, the Battle of unayn, and the march to Tabk. He was also among the soldiers of the army of Sayyidun Usmah ibn Zayd   . 

After returning from this expedition, he volunteered in the wars against apostasy and had the honor of participating in the Battle of Yammah against Musaylamah al-Kadhdhb. 

He later marched to Shm and fought at al-Yarmk, then joined the campaigns in the Persian lands under Sayyidun Sad ibn Ab Waqq   , attending al-Qdisiyyah, Jall, and the conquest of al-Madin. He also took part in the conquest of Egypt and campaigns in North Africa. 

Even in his later years, he joined the expedition toward Constantinople, about which the Prophet  said: The first army to attack the city of Caesar is forgiven.

What distinguished Ibn Umar    above all was his extraordinary commitment to following the Sunnah. He would carefully observe where the Prophet  prayed, where he sat, and even the paths he walked, then imitate them with remarkable precision. 

If he saw the Prophet  perform prayer in a particular place, he would later pray in that very same place. If he saw him make supplication while standing, he would supplicate while standing; and if he saw him invoke Allh while sitting, he would do the same. His life was not merely one of narration, but of complete imitation.

He was among the foremost scholars of the Companions and a prolific narrator of hadith, transmitting over 2,600 narrations. His knowledge was paired with deep caution, he was known for his reluctance to issue legal verdicts without certainty, and he preferred silence over speculation. 

Due to his age, knowledge, and unwavering adherence to the Sunnah, he became known in his time as the Shaykh of the abah, and even powerful governors such as al-ajjj ibn Ysuf treated him with respect and deference due to his immense status amongst the Muslims. From the greatest testimonies to his precision in transmitting the Sunnah is that he stands at the head of one of the most authentic chains in hadith, the Golden Chain.

Mlik  Nfi  Ibn Umar.

A generation of leading Tbin took knowledge from him, including Sad ibn al-Musayyib, Thbit al-Bunn, ws, al-Zuhr, Muammad ibn Srn, Nfi (his freed servant), Mujhid, and many others. His influence thus extended deeply into the next generation of scholarship. 

His legacy was also carried forward through his own household, as several of his sons became scholars, most notably Slim ibn Abdullh ibn Umar, who was counted among the great jurists of Madnah. Through both his students and his family, his knowledge and methodology continued to shape the transmission of the Sunnah for generations.

During the periods of civil strife, having lived to witness both the First and Second Fitnah among the Muslims, Ibn Umar    consistently adopted a principled stance of restraint. He presented himself at the sittings of arbitration, demonstrating his desire for unity among the Muslims. 

He famously said: When you call me to prayer, I respond. However, when you call me to fight a Muslim, I do not. He never raised his sword against the believers. Yet, on the other hand, he answered every call to strive against the disbelievers, embodying the words of Allh, Most High:

Humble toward the believers, powerful against the disbelievers; they strive in the cause of Allh 

[Qurn 5:54]

He was known for his humility, generosity, and detachment from worldly life. Wealth would come to him, yet he would spend it freely in charity. He freed many slaves and cared deeply for the poor and needy. His nights were often spent in prayer, and his days in fasting and remembrance.

Sayyidun Abdullh ibn Umar    passed away in Makkah in 73 AH and was buried near Dh uw, leaving behind a legacy of unwavering adherence to the Sunnah and a life defined by sincerity and caution.

May Allh be pleased with Sayyidun Abdullh ibn Umar   , the devoted follower of the Sunnah and one of the greatest scholars among the Companions of the Messenger of Allh ."

" The bid'ah of the Murji'ah is more harmful than that of the Khawarij. 

The scholars of Ahl al-Sunnah warned strongly about the bid'ah of Irja, to the extent that many of the Salaf considered it more dangerous than the innovation of the Khawarij.

Why?

 The Khawarij reach a limit in misguidance, but the Murji'ah have no boundary; they gradually strip the religion away, piece by piece.

 The Murji'ah open the door to zandaqah (heresy) and disbelief, by emptying iman of action.

 They corrupt the understanding of the religion while appearing outwardly agreeable, even aligning with rulers and worldly interests.

 They widen the door to false interpretations, distorting the texts without restraint.

 They give false security to sinners, claiming salvation despite wrongdoing, resembling the Jews who said:

  "The Fire will not touch us except for a number of days." (al-Baqarah: 80)

Because of this, many of the Salaf (such as al-Nakha'i) said:

"Fitnat al-Murji'ah is more feared upon this Ummah than that of the Azariqah (extreme Khawarij)."

And others said:

"The Khawarij are more excusable to me than the Murji'ah."

The reality is that both groups are harmful, but:

 The Khawarij corrupt through extremism and rebellion.
 The Murjiah corrupt the very foundation of iman itself.

And corruption of the foundation is more severe.

 al-Khorasaniya - Shaykh 'Abd al-Aziz al-Turayfi"
